biography.father: Martin Schneier — Brooklyn Supreme Court judge
biography.personal: Married Karen Cooper 1997; divorced 2022; resided Minneapolis during marriage; honorary Ph.D. University of Westminster 2011

career_timeline.description: Early cryptography work for government and defense; developed foundational technical depth in applied cryptography
Laid off; began writing for computer magazines; pivot that launched public career
Published through John Wiley; 'launched me as an expert in this field'; first comprehensive practical cryptography book for practitioners

career_timeline.role: Co-founder and CTO, Counterpane Internet Security
career_timeline.description: Managed security monitoring services; evolved his thinking from 'security as product' to 'security as process'
Counterpane acquired; Schneier continued as BT Security Evangelist

career_timeline.role: CTO, Resilient Systems (later acquired by IBM 2016)
career_timeline.description: Incident response platform; departed IBM June 2019

career_timeline.role: Fellow, Berkman Klein Center, Harvard; Adjunct Lecturer, Harvard Kennedy School
career_timeline.description: Security policy, democracy, and AI governance; public policy dimension of security work

career_timeline.role: Board member: EFF, Tor Project, Access Now; advisor to EPIC, VerifiedVoting.org
career_timeline.description: Translates technical security expertise into policy and civil liberties advocacy

major_books.title: Applied Cryptography
major_books.argument: First comprehensive practitioner guide to cryptographic algorithms; established Schneier as the field's translator between academic cryptography and practical implementation

major_books.title: Secrets and Lies: Digital Security in a Networked World
major_books.argument: Security requires process, not just products. Technology alone cannot solve security problems. Risk management is continuous, not episodic.

major_books.title: Beyond Fear: Thinking Sensibly About Security in an Uncertain World
major_books.argument: Security decisions should be made by comparing risks, costs, and trade-offs — not by emotional response to dramatic threats. The 'movie-plot threat' problem.

major_books.title: Liars and Outliers: Enabling the Trust That Society Needs to Thrive
major_books.argument: Trust is the mechanism that makes civilization function; defectors force costly societal responses; cooperative norms require institutional enforcement.

major_books.title: Data and Goliath
major_books.argument: Surveillance capitalism and government data collection represent hidden battles over control. Collection without transparency is a power asymmetry problem.

major_books.title: Click Here to Kill Everybody
major_books.argument: Internet-connected physical systems (the 'Internet of Things') creates catastrophic security risks because failures are no longer digital — they are physical. Requires government regulation.

major_books.title: A Hacker's Mind
major_books.argument: Hacking — exploiting system rules in unintended ways — is a universal phenomenon. Powerful entities hack democracy, tax codes, and financial markets. AI makes this hacking faster and more dangerous. Understanding it is the first step to redesigning fairer systems.

major_books.title: Rewiring Democracy (with Nathan Sanders)
major_books.argument: AI transforms politics and governance. Covers AI's impact on elections, citizenship, and the power balance between institutions and individuals.

cryptographic_work.note: Twofish was AES finalist; Skein was SHA-3 finalist. Schneier's own algorithms tested by global cryptanalysis community — practicing what he preaches about peer review.

documented_positions.surveillance: Data collection without analysis improvement doesn't enhance security; mass surveillance fails at counterterrorism because intelligence analysis quality matters more than data volume; 'It is poor civic hygiene to install technologies that could someday facilitate a police state.'
documented_positions.cryptography: Mathematical weakness rarely the actual vulnerability; implementation and integration matter more than algorithm; Kerckhoffs's Principle — good security works even when design is public
documented_positions.airport_security: Post-9/11 changes caused 'more harm than good'; defeated TSA head Kip Hawley in Economist debate 87% to 13%
documented_positions.drm: Enables vendor lock-in without improving security; 'Digital files cannot be made uncopyable, any more than water can be made not wet'
documented_positions.ai_security: AI makes hacking faster and at inhuman scale; AI in government requires transparency; AI cybersecurity creates new attack surfaces faster than it closes old ones
documented_positions.power_and_hacking: Powerful entities exploit system loopholes as a form of institutionalized hacking; this is the defining security challenge of the 21st century beyond technical attacks
documented_positions.data_minimization: Data is a toxic asset; 'There's no better security than deleting the data'

notable_defeats_and_wins.event: Economist debate on airport security vs. TSA head Kip Hawley
notable_defeats_and_wins.outcome: Won 87% to 13% — documented public win on record
notable_defeats_and_wins.significance: His 'security theater' argument validated by public opinion against a sitting government official

notable_defeats_and_wins.outcome: Widely adopted; never broken; set the standard for open cryptographic contribution
notable_defeats_and_wins.significance: Practices what he preaches: transparency over obscurity

notable_defeats_and_wins.outcome: Did not win (AES selected Rijndael), but survived global cryptanalysis scrutiny
notable_defeats_and_wins.significance: Public peer review as the ultimate validation mechanism
